title: playerz.bg — service health
description: The numbers that mean something. Panels are ordered by what you look at FIRST during an incident: is money moving, are bookings landing, is the system slow. Everything below the fold is diagnosis, not detection.

panels.title: Payment failures (by reason)
panels.description: This is the first panel because it is the first thing that matters. 'card_declined' is the customer's problem; 'payouts_not_enabled' is ours. They are split BECAUSE averaging them into one line would hide our bug behind their declines.

panels.title: Pending payout (unsettled money owed to venues)
panels.description: A gauge that only ever goes up is a gauge telling you transfers are silently failing and clubs are not being paid. That is normally discovered by an angry phone call.

panels.title: Double-bookings rejected
panels.description: THIS IS NOT AN ERROR PANEL. A rejection is the EXCLUDE constraint doing its job — two people raced for one court and Postgres refused the loser. A steady trickle is healthy. A sudden spike means a popular slot, a client retrying in a loop, or a bug hammering us — and it is one of the few numbers that tells you something is wrong BEFORE a user does. Do not alert on the count; alert on the slope.

panels.title: HTTP latency (p50 / p95 / p99)
panels.description: p95, not the mean. A mean of 200ms is perfectly compatible with one user in twenty waiting four seconds — and that user is the one who leaves.

panels.title: Oldest job in queue (age, not depth)
panels.description: Depth lies. A thousand fast jobs is fine; ten stuck ones is not. If the oldest job in the reminder queue is two hours old, somebody's booking reminder will arrive after their booking.
